ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'chicken chop with sauce' as a composite dish; mapped to chicken thigh food product as the primary protein component. Sauce component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (chicken meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor. No composite dish code exists for chicken chop with sauce in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken chop with sauce as a prepared composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of chicken chop with sauce is approximately 200g, comprising one grilled or pan-fried chicken thigh with skin and a portion of sauce, as typically served in Singaporean Western food stalls and coffeeshops.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Chop with Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Chicken chop with sauce' (CR ID: F11020014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 05170, chicken thigh with skin, cooked, roasted) as HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; value adapted proportionally.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed protein-sauce dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'chicken chop with sauce' in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed protein-dominant dishes with small sauce carbohydrate contribution (~5g/100g carbohydrate, predominantly from sauce). The protein content (19.2g/100g) substantially blunts glycemic response. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (35 × 5.0 × 2.0) / 100 = 3.5g, classified as low GL. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 466mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold; a standard 200g serving delivers approximately 932mg sodium, representing 40% of the 2300mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request sauce on the side and use it sparingly, as the sauce is the primary sodium driver; choosing grilled over pan-fried preparation and avoiding additional table salt or soy sauce can reduce sodium intake by an estimated 30–40%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 5g/100g with sugar at 4g/100g; high protein at 19.2g/100g slows gastric emptying and blunts post-meal glucose excursion, yielding an estimated GI of 35 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken chop with sauce is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in standard portions when paired with non-starchy vegetables; patients should avoid sweet sauces such as honey mustard or BBQ sauce and request sauce on the side to control sugar and sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 11.3g/100g with saturated fat approximately 3.1g/100g and estimated trans fat 0.1g/100g from pan-frying; sodium at 466mg/100g is also a cardiovascular risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request grilled rather than pan-fried preparation to eliminate trans fat and reduce total fat, choose sauce on the side to limit sodium, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 466mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g RA threshold for tissue swelling; estimated trans fat 0.1g/100g from pan-frying is a pro-inflammatory disqualifier; dish lacks meaningful anti-inflammatory components.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request grilled preparation to eliminate trans fat and reduce advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), ask for sauce on the side to reduce sodium, and add a side of anti-inflammatory vegetables such as broccoli or spinach.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Chop with Sauce is a popular Singaporean Western-style dish consisting of a grilled or pan-fried chicken thigh with skin, served with a savoury sauce — typically brown sauce, mushroom sauce, or black pepper sauce. It is a staple at Singaporean coffeeshop Western food stalls and hawker centres, representing the local Eurasian-influenced adaptation of Western grilled chicken dishes. The chicken thigh is seasoned, cooked until the skin is lightly crisped, and plated with a generous pour of sauce. The texture is juicy and tender with a slightly charred exterior, and the sauce adds umami depth and mild sweetness. Nutritionally, the dish provides 198 kcal per 100g, with high protein at 19.2g/100g making it a protein-dense meal option. Fat content is moderate at 11.3g/100g, contributed by the chicken skin and cooking oil. Carbohydrate is low at 5g/100g, primarily from the sauce. Sodium is elevated at 466mg/100g, driven by the sauce and seasoning, which is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and high protein profile, but sodium management is critical. It is commonly served with coleslaw, baked beans, and fries or rice in the Singaporean context.
common_user_queries: Is chicken chop with sauce healthy?
How many calories in chicken chop with sauce?
Is chicken chop suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat chicken chop if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of chicken chop with sauce?
What sauce comes with chicken chop in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for 'Chicken chop with sauce' (CR ID: F11020014) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05170 (chicken thigh with skin, cooked, roasted) as the closest approved-source equivalent for fatty acid and micronutrient detail not reported in the HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Overall confidence is high given the exact HPB match for primary macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.1g/100g), monounsaturated fat (4.4g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (2.4g/100g), trans fat (0.1g/100g), potassium (290mg/100g), and cholesterol (85mg/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05170 (chicken thigh with skin, cooked, roasted) as HPB Foodo does not separately report these values for this dish. Values are proportionally consistent with the HPB-reported total fat of 11.3g/100g. 2. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish (chicken thigh with sauce) are not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database, and the sauce component introduces variability that makes direct USDA substitution unreliable for these fields. 3. Trans fat estimated at 0.1g/100g based on pan-frying preparation method; this is a pro-inflammatory concern for rheumatoid arthritis patients. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Keto dietary tag from input retained with caution — the 5g/100g carbohydrate is borderline for strict ketogenic thresholds depending on sauce type and portion size. 6. All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from HPB input. Added multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Singaporean dish compliance. Added saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05170 as approved secondary source. Set GI to 35 (analogous estimate, low) with University of Sydney GI Database framework citation. Added all three required health_context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Expanded aliases and aliases_by_language. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Verified aliases consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases array.

Self-scored draft — all 8 criteria pass, all 6 hard gates pass. Score 8/8. Final decision PASS confirmed.
